steps.title: The People You're Becoming
steps.body:
Here's a truth that's rarely said out loud: you will not be the same person in 10 years. Neither will your partner.

Life changes you. Experiences reshape you. Priorities shift.

The question is: will you grow together or grow apart?

Some couples grow apart because they assume growth is automatic. They stop checking in. They stop learning about each other.

Other couples grow together because they make it intentional. They stay curious. They ask: how are you changing? What do you need that's new? What do you want to become?

Today is about making growth a shared project — not something that happens to you, but something you do together.

The person you married is not the person they will become.

And that's not a problem.
It's an invitation.

The question is: will you get to know each new version — or wake up one day next to a stranger?
highlights: not the person they will become
get to know each new version

questions.label:
Think about yourself five years ago. How were you different?

• What mattered to you then that matters less now?
• What do you care about now that you didn't then?
• How have your values, priorities, or perspectives shifted?

Now think about your partner — how have THEY changed?

Have you been tracking their evolution? Have they been tracking yours?

steps.title: The Growth Check-In
steps.body:
Here's a simple practice: every six months, sit down and share how you're changing.

Questions you can ask each other:

• How have I changed in the last six months — for better or worse?
• What's something new you've learned about yourself?
• What do you want more of in your life that you didn't want six months ago?
• What do you want less of?
• How can I support the person you're becoming?

This isn't a heavy conversation. It's a way of staying curious. Of not assuming you know everything about your partner.

Because the worst feeling in a marriage isn't fighting. It's realizing you've become strangers.

steps.question: True growth as a couple means:
options: Always changing in the same direction at the same time
Supporting each other's individual growth while maintaining connection
Sacrificing your own growth for the stability of the marriage
Growing independently and hoping you stay aligned
feedback: That's not realistic. You'll grow at different paces and in different directions. The key is connection through the difference.
Yes. Growth together means each person evolves while the relationship stays connected and curious. You don't have to change in the same way — just stay in each other's lives as you change.
Sacrificing growth leads to resentment. A healthy marriage supports both partners' development.
Hope is not a strategy. You need intentional check-ins to stay connected through change.

questions.label:
Think about your partner's growth over the last year.

• What new interests have they developed?
• What are they wrestling with?
• What do they seem to want MORE of in their life?
• What's one thing about them that's still unfolding that you want to stay close to?

If you can't answer these, that's information — not guilt. It means you have more to learn about each other.

steps.title: Careers Change Everything
steps.body:
You married someone who was ambitious about their career. Five years later, they want to step back and focus on family. Or vice versa.

This isn't a betrayal. It's growth.

The danger isn't that their priorities shifted. The danger is that you don't talk about it, so you operate on outdated assumptions about who they are and what they want.

Check in regularly. 'Are we still the same people we were when we set our life plan? If not, how should the plan change?'

steps.title: Schedule Your Growth Check-In
steps.prompt:
Schedule a 30-minute growth check-in with your partner this week.

Here's your invite:

'I heard about this idea of a growth check-in — asking each other how we're changing and what we need. Can we do that this week over coffee? Just 30 minutes.'

Write when and where you'll do it:
steps.body:

Here's your invite:

'I heard about this idea of a growth check-in — asking each other how we're changing and what we need. Can we do that this week over coffee? Just 30 minutes.'

Write when and where you'll do it:

steps.question: If a couple grows in different directions, it always means the relationship is in trouble.
feedback: Correct. Growing in different directions is normal. What creates trouble is growing silently — not communicating about it, not staying curious, not finding ways to stay connected through the change.
Not quite. Growth in different directions is natural. The danger isn't the different direction — it's the silence that lets the distance grow unnoticed.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Becoming a Team That Wins Together
steps.body: You've learned to grow together through change. Tomorrow, we take it further — how to function as a real team. Not just people who share a life, but partners who strategize, support, and celebrate together.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
